caption.case_title: Complainant v. Housing Authority of Clackamas County
caption.caption_note: Retaliation complaint anchored to HACC core housing policies

parties.plaintiff: Complainant / tenant or program participant (name to be inserted).

jurisdiction_and_venue: Jurisdiction and venue should be tailored to the final filing forum and the specific legal claims asserted.
The current draft is grounded in HACC housing-policy evidence and is intended as a complaint-development scaffold rather than a filed pleading.
The dispute appears to arise from housing-program administration, adverse action, and procedural protections connected to HACC operations.
factual_allegations: The complaint centers on a retaliation and grievance-related housing complaint involving HACC notice and review protections.
The complainant contends that the grievance or hearing process was not handled in the manner described by HACC policy.
The complainant contends that appeal rights and due-process protections were not clearly honored.
The core documentary support for these allegations appears in Exhibit A (ADMISSIONS AND CONTINUED OCCUPANCY POLICY) and Exhibit B (ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN).
claims_theory: Fair Housing Act anti-retaliation provisions, 24 C.F.R. Part 100 may be implicated if HACC used grievance, review, or adverse-action procedures to respond to protected complaints or protected activity.

The available policy language suggests the complainant should have received an informal review or hearing, written notice, and a review decision, but the intake narrative describes those protections as missing or unclear.
The complainant also describes a retaliation theory based on the timing of the adverse treatment after protected complaints or grievance activity.

The policy theory is grounded in HACC language stating that If HACC denies a request for an accommodation because it is not reasonable (it would impose an undue financial and administrative burden or fundamentally alter the nature of HACC’s operations), HACC will notify the family in writing of its decision. The notice will inform the family of the right to appeal HACC’s decision through an informal hearing (if ap... That documentary support is reflected in Exhibit A (ADMISSIONS AND CONTINUED OCCUPANCY POLICY) and Exhibit B (ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN).
policy_basis: ADMISSIONS AND CONTINUED OCCUPANCY POLICY supports grievance hearing, appeal rights: [reasonable_accommodation, notice, hearing] If HACC denies a request for an accommodation because it is not reasonable (it would impose an undue financial and administrative burden or fundamentally alter the nature of HACC’s operations), HACC will notify the family in writing of its decision. The notice will inform the family of the right to appeal HACC’s decision through an informal hearing (if ap... Full passage: If HACC denies a request for an accommodation because it is not reasonable (it would impose an undue financial and administrative burden or fundamentally alter the nature of HACC’s operations), HACC will notify the family in writing of its decision. The notice will inform the family of the right to appeal HACC’s decision through an informal hearing (if applicable) or the grievance process (see Chapter 14). If HACC denies a request for an accommodation because it is not reasonable (it would impose an undue financial and administrative burden or fundamentally alter the nature of HACC’s operations), but has determined there is an alternative accommodation that could effectively address the family's disability related need without a fundamental alteration to the public housing program without imposing an undue financial and administrative burden, HACC will notify the family in writing.

ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN supports grievance hearing, appeal rights: [hearing] Informal Hearings for Participants), an opportunity for an informal hearing must be given to residents for any dispute that a resident may have with respect to an owner action in accordance with the individual’s lease or the contract administrator in accordance with RAD PBV requirements that adversely affect the resident’s rights, obligations, welfare, or... Full passage: Informal Hearings for Participants), an opportunity for an informal hearing must be given to residents for any dispute that a resident may have with respect to an owner action in accordance with the individual’s lease or the contract administrator in accordance with RAD PBV requirements that adversely affect the resident’s rights, obligations, welfare, or status.  For any hearing required under 24 CFR 982.555(a)(1)(i)–(v), the contract administrator will perform the hearing in accordance with Chapter 16 Part III: Informal Reviews and Hearings, as is the current standard in the program.  For any additional hearings required under RAD, HACC (as owner) will perform the hearing. An informal hearing will not be required for class grievances or for disputes between residents not involving HACC (as owner) or contract administrator. This hearing requirement does not apply to and is not intended as a forum for initiating or negotiating policy changes between a group or groups of residents and HACC (as owner) or contract administrator.

causes_of_action.title: Failure to Provide Required Notice and Process
causes_of_action.theory: The draft facts suggest denial or termination activity without the clear notice, review, or hearing process described by HACC policy.
support: Fair Housing Act anti-retaliation provisions, 24 C.F.R. Part 100 may be implicated if HACC used grievance, review, or adverse-action procedures to respond to protected complaints or protected activity.

The available policy language suggests the complainant should have received an informal review or hearing, written notice, and a review decision, but the intake narrative describes those protections as missing or unclear.
Documentary support: Exhibit A (ADMISSIONS AND CONTINUED OCCUPANCY POLICY). Rationale: selected as the closest documentary match for this claim.
causes_of_action.selection_rationale: selected as the closest documentary match for this claim

causes_of_action.title: Retaliation for Protected Complaint Activity
causes_of_action.theory: The complainant narrative suggests adverse treatment after raising concerns or invoking grievance protections.
support: Fair Housing Act anti-retaliation provisions, 24 C.F.R. Part 100 may be implicated if HACC used grievance, review, or adverse-action procedures to respond to protected complaints or protected activity.
The complainant also describes a retaliation theory based on the timing of the adverse treatment after protected complaints or grievance activity.

Documentary support: Exhibit A (ADMISSIONS AND CONTINUED OCCUPANCY POLICY) and Exhibit B (ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN). Rationale: selected for the strongest overlap with grievance activity and adverse-process protections tied to the retaliation theory.
causes_of_action.selection_rationale: selected for the strongest overlap with grievance activity and adverse-process protections tied to the retaliation theory

proposed_allegations: Plaintiff alleges conduct arising from a retaliation and grievance-related housing complaint involving HACC notice and review protections.

The available HACC materials indicate that If HACC denies a request for an accommodation because it is not reasonable (it would impose an undue financial and administrative burden or fundamentally alter the nature of HACC’s operations), HACC will notify the family in writing of its decision. The notice will inform the family of the right to appeal HACC’s decision through an informal hearing (if ap...
The intake record suggests the grievance or hearing process was not handled in the manner described by HACC policy.

Case-specific facts still need confirmation, including the date and nature of the adverse action, whether written notice, an informal review, a grievance hearing, or an appeal was requested or denied, who at HACC made or communicated the decision, the resulting housing harm and requested remedy.
requested_relief: Declaratory relief identifying the challenged conduct and policies.
Injunctive relief requiring fair review, corrected process, and non-retaliation safeguards.
Compensatory damages or other available monetary relief according to proof.
Costs, fees, and any other relief authorized by law.
